note_data.title: Solutions
keyPoints: Solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances — solvent is present in larger amount, solute in smaller amount
Solutions can be gaseous (air), liquid (sugar in water), or solid (alloys like brass)
Concentration can be expressed as percentage by mass, percentage by volume, or molarity
Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in 100g of solvent at a given temperature
Saturated solution contains maximum amount of solute at given temperature — no more solute can dissolve
Unsaturated solution can dissolve more solute, supersaturated contains more solute than normal solubility
Henry's Law states that solubility of gas in liquid is directly proportional to pressure of gas
Crystallization is the process of obtaining pure crystals from saturated solution by cooling or evaporation

definitions.definition: A solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances in which the particle size is less than 1 nm
The component of solution which is present in larger amount and dissolves the other component
The component of solution which is present in smaller amount and gets dissolved in solvent
A solution which contains maximum amount of solute that can be dissolved in given amount of solvent at a particular temperature
The maximum amount of solute in grams that can dissolve in 100g of solvent at a given temperature to form saturated solution

importantQuestions.question: Define saturated and unsaturated solutions with examples.
importantQuestions.answer: Saturated solution contains maximum amount of solute at given temperature (e.g., no more salt dissolves in water). Unsaturated solution can dissolve more solute (e.g., sugar can still dissolve in tea).

importantQuestions.question: Explain different types of solutions with examples. How does temperature affect solubility?
importantQuestions.answer: Types: (1) Gaseous solutions - air (oxygen in nitrogen), (2) Liquid solutions - seawater (salt in water), (3) Solid solutions - brass (zinc in copper). Temperature effect: For solids, solubility increases with temperature. For gases, solubility decreases with temperature. Example: sugar dissolves more in hot water, CO2 escapes from warm soft drinks.

mcqs.question: Which of following is example of liquid in liquid solution?
Solubility of gases in liquids decreases with increase in:
A solution containing 25g NaCl in 100g water at 25°C is:

commonMistakes: Students confuse solute and solvent — remember solvent is in larger amount
Writing percentage by mass formula incorrectly — always write (mass of solute/mass of solution) × 100
Forgetting that solubility of gases decreases with temperature increase
Not mentioning temperature when defining saturated solution
Confusing saturated with concentrated solution — they are different concepts
examTips: This chapter usually has 2-3 MCQs and 1 short question in every BSEK paper
Always define terms exactly as given in STBB textbook — examiners expect word-to-word definitions
Memorize the solubility curve diagram — frequently asked to draw and explain
Practice percentage calculations — numerical problems are common in 5-mark questions
formulas: Percentage by mass = (Mass of solute / Mass of solution) × 100
Percentage by volume = (Volume of solute / Volume of solution) × 100
Mass of solution = Mass of solute + Mass of solvent
note_data.keyDiagram: Solubility curve showing how solubility of different salts (KNO3, NaCl, KCl) varies with temperature. X-axis shows temperature in °C, Y-axis shows solubility in g/100g water. Must show KNO3 having steepest curve and NaCl having gentlest slope.
